prevalence.source: Indian sleep epidemiology studies + WHO context
prevalence.trend: increasing with urbanization, shift work, and stress

burden.mortalityRate: Negligible (indirect via accidents and metabolic risk)

epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Insomnia disorder criteria with daytime impairment; rule out OSA and other sleep disorders; somatic complaints may predominate

publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Low to moderate; CBT-I limited and sedative use varies

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Primary care evaluation often limited; sleep labs concentrated in urban tertiary centers
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Sleep hygiene and CBT-I where available; short-term hypnotics; address comorbid anxiety/depression and OSA when suspected
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Limited CBT-I access, high self-medication, and low specialist sleep medicine availability
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Integration of lifestyle approaches (yoga/meditation) and expanding telehealth services for counseling
